title_seed: What I learned while deploying ML models with FastAPI and Docker
experience_points: Deployment is more than just creating an API endpoint
Input validation with Pydantic catches problems before they reach the model
Docker keeps the environment stable across machines and CI
Health check endpoints matter when you're running in containers
mistakes: I ignored proper error handling at first and the API crashed silently
I did not pin library versions in requirements.txt and the container broke weeks later
I forgot to add a /health endpoint and had no way to check if the container was alive
lessons: Production readiness needs validation, logging, and versioning from day one
Always test your Docker image locally before pushing to any registry
Your model artifact path should come from an environment variable, not hardcoded
optional_code_snippet_idea: simple FastAPI prediction endpoint with Pydantic input model and error handling

title_seed: How MLflow changed the way I track ML experiments
experience_points: Before MLflow I was saving metrics in print statements and notebooks
MLflow autolog works for sklearn and XGBoost out of the box — no extra code
The model registry lets you move models from staging to production with a single API call
Comparing runs side by side in the UI saves hours of manual spreadsheet work
mistakes: I ran 50 experiments without naming them and could not figure out which one was best later
I did not log the random seed and could not reproduce my best result
I logged too many metrics and the UI became cluttered and hard to read
lessons: Log the essentials: params, metrics, model artifact, and the dataset version
Use meaningful run names — you will thank yourself later
Reproducibility requires logging everything that affects the output
optional_code_snippet_idea: mlflow.start_run() block with param logging, metric logging, and model registration

title_seed: Mistakes I made while training my first ML model
experience_points: I trained on the full dataset without a proper train/test split
I did not check for data leakage and got suspiciously high accuracy
Scaling features made a huge difference for models like SVM and KNN
I used accuracy as the only metric on an imbalanced dataset — it was meaningless
mistakes: My test set contained rows that influenced the scaler fit — classic leakage
I never looked at the confusion matrix and shipped a model that missed all minority class samples
I tuned hyperparameters on the test set instead of using cross-validation
lessons: Always split data before any preprocessing step
Use multiple metrics: precision, recall, F1, and AUC for imbalanced problems
Cross-validation is not optional — it is the minimum standard
optional_code_snippet_idea: sklearn Pipeline with StandardScaler, train_test_split, and cross_val_score

title_seed: Why model monitoring matters more than model training
experience_points: A model that performs well in testing can silently degrade in production
Data drift is the most common reason models stop working after deployment
Prometheus and Grafana together give you real-time visibility into model behavior
Setting up prediction distribution monitoring caught a data pipeline bug before users noticed
mistakes: I thought deployment was the finish line — I had no monitoring set up for weeks
I only monitored infrastructure metrics, not model-specific metrics like prediction confidence
I had no alerting — I found out about drift from a user complaint, not a dashboard
lessons: Monitor input data distributions, not just model outputs
Set up alerting on day one of deployment, not when something breaks
Treat model monitoring as a first-class engineering concern
optional_code_snippet_idea: custom Prometheus counter and histogram for prediction tracking in FastAPI

title_seed: How I set up a CI/CD pipeline for ML models using GitHub Actions
experience_points: GitHub Actions is free for public repos and runs on a generous quota for private ones
I automated model validation checks before any merge to main
The pipeline runs unit tests, linting, and a smoke test on the model artifact
Environment variables as GitHub Secrets kept credentials out of the codebase
mistakes: I committed my .env file to the repo early on — had to rotate all credentials
My workflow had no caching and took 8 minutes to install dependencies every run
I did not test the workflow on a feature branch and it broke main on the first merge
lessons: Use actions/cache for pip dependencies — it cuts 70% of runtime
Always test workflows on a branch before merging
A failing CI pipeline is better than a broken deployment
optional_code_snippet_idea: GitHub Actions workflow with pip cache, pytest run, and Docker build step

title_seed: What I learned about feature engineering that no tutorial tells you
experience_points: Good features matter more than a fancy model in most real-world problems
Domain knowledge is more valuable than any automated feature selection algorithm
Interaction features between columns sometimes reveal patterns tree models miss
Target encoding on high-cardinality columns consistently outperformed one-hot in my experiments
mistakes: I one-hot encoded a column with 500 unique values and blew up training time
I created features using the full dataset before splitting — data leakage again
I spent days on feature engineering when the real problem was bad data quality
lessons: Understand your data before engineering features — EDA first, always
Simpler features that make business sense often beat complex engineered ones
Track which features actually help using feature importance or permutation tests
optional_code_snippet_idea: pandas pipeline for target encoding with cross-validation to prevent leakage

title_seed: Running background tasks in Python with Celery and Redis — what I wish I knew
experience_points: Celery decouples heavy work from your web request cycle completely
Redis as a broker is the easiest setup — one line of config and it works
Task chaining lets you build pipelines where each step triggers the next
Flower gave me a real-time view of task queues without any extra work
mistakes: I forgot to configure task serialization and got silent failures on complex objects
I ran workers with the default concurrency and overloaded a small VM
I did not set task timeouts and a hung task blocked the queue for hours
lessons: Always set task_time_limit and soft_time_limit for every task
Log task IDs — you will need them to trace failures in production
Test your tasks as regular Python functions first before wiring Celery in
optional_code_snippet_idea: Celery task definition with retry logic, timeout, and result backend configuration

title_seed: How I structured a real ML project — folders, configs, and lessons from the mess
experience_points: Flat scripts work for notebooks but fall apart when you add a second teammate
Separating data loading, feature engineering, and modeling into modules made refactoring easy
Config files replaced hardcoded paths and made the project portable across machines
A proper .gitignore saved me from accidentally committing 2 GB of data files
mistakes: I put all code in one 800-line script and debugging became a nightmare
I used absolute paths everywhere and the project broke on every other machine
I had no logging and spent hours adding print statements to find a bug
lessons: Use a src layout or at minimum an app/ folder from the very start
Configuration belongs in .env or YAML files, not in source code
Logging is not optional — add it before you need it
optional_code_snippet_idea: recommended ML project folder structure with config.py pattern using python-dotenv

title_seed: Understanding model drift and how I set up automated retraining
experience_points: Concept drift happens when the relationship between features and labels changes over time
Data drift happens when the input distribution shifts — the model sees new patterns it was not trained on
I used statistical tests like KS-test and PSI to detect drift in production data
Automated retraining triggered by drift detection reduced manual intervention completely
mistakes: I retrained on a schedule regardless of drift — wasted compute and sometimes made the model worse
I did not version the retraining data and could not debug a regression after a retrain
I confused data drift with concept drift and used the wrong detection method
lessons: Drift detection should trigger retraining, not a calendar schedule
Always version your training datasets alongside model artifacts
Shadow deployment is the safest way to validate a retrained model before switching
optional_code_snippet_idea: KS-test drift detection script that reads production prediction logs and compares to training distribution

title_seed: PostgreSQL for ML engineers — storing features, predictions, and logs properly
experience_points: A proper predictions table gave me the audit trail I needed to debug model issues
Feature stores backed by PostgreSQL kept training and serving features consistent
SQLAlchemy ORM made switching between SQLite for local dev and Postgres in production seamless
Indexing on timestamp and user_id columns made prediction lookup queries 10x faster
mistakes: I stored JSON blobs in a text column instead of using PostgreSQL jsonb — queries were painfully slow
I did not add timestamps to my tables and lost the ability to filter by time range
I ran raw string queries with user input and had a SQL injection vulnerability in a test endpoint
lessons: Use jsonb not text for JSON data in PostgreSQL — it is indexable
Always include created_at and updated_at columns in every table from the start
Parameterized queries are non-negotiable — never concatenate user input into SQL
optional_code_snippet_idea: SQLAlchemy model for a predictions table with jsonb input features, predicted label, confidence score, and timestamp
